title: Sickle Cell Disease - Acute Chest Syndrome
chief_complaint: My son has sickle cell disease. He's had a pain crisis for 2 days, and now he's having trouble breathing, chest pain, and a fever. His oxygen levels are low.

physical_exam.Lungs: Tachypnea, retractions. Crackles and decreased breath sounds in right lower lobe. Dullness to percussion right base.
physical_exam.Chest: Chest wall tenderness (rib infarction from vaso-occlusion).
physical_exam.Abdomen: Mild tenderness. Spleen tip palpable (autosplenectomy not yet complete at this age).

hidden_data.cxr: New right lower lobe infiltrate. Small right pleural effusion. No cardiomegaly.
hidden_data.cbc: Hb 5.5 (baseline 8.0 - significant drop), WBC 22,000, Platelets 180,000. Reticulocyte count 15% (appropriately elevated).
hidden_data.abg: pH 7.38, PaCO2 42, PaO2 55, SaO2 83% on room air
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 1.0, BUN 18, LDH 850 (elevated - hemolysis), Total Bilirubin 5.5, Direct 0.8
hidden_data.echo: Normal LV function. Moderate TR jet (pulmonary hypertension from chronic hemolysis).

correct_diagnosis: Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS) in Sickle Cell Disease (HbSS)
key_learning_points: ACS = leading cause of death in SCD. Defined as: New pulmonary infiltrate on CXR + Fever and/or respiratory symptoms + Hypoxia. Can progress rapidly to respiratory failure

Causes: Infection (Chlamydia pneumoniae, Mycoplasma, S. pneumoniae), Pulmonary vaso-occlusion (sickled RBCs occlude pulmonary microvasculature → infarction), Fat embolism from bone marrow infarction, Rib infarction causing splinting and atelectasis. Often multifactorial

ACS can develop during VOC (pain crisis). Hypoventilation from rib pain + opioid-induced respiratory depression → atelectasis → hypoxia → more sickling → vicious cycle. INCENTIVE SPIROMETRY prevents ACS during VOC

Treatment: Oxygen (target SpO2 >95%), Antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin - covers typical and atypical organisms), Pain control (Morphine PCA, but monitor for hypoventilation), IV fluids (D5½NS at 1-1.5× maintenance - hypotonic to prevent pulmonary edema), Incentive spirometry q2h while awake

Simple transfusion if Hb <6 or drop >2g/dL from baseline. Exchange transfusion (erythrocytapheresis) if: Severe ACS (rapidly progressive, requiring >50% FiO2), Multi-organ failure, or Hb >9 (too viscous for simple transfusion). Target Hb 10-11 and HbS <30%

Bronchodilators if wheezing. Corticosteroids controversial (reduce duration but increase risk of rebound VOC). PICU if severe respiratory distress or mechanical ventilation required. Mortality: 1-3% per episode. 80% of patients with ACS have recurrent episodes
